text.title: Semantic Tokens and Modes Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the semantic-tokens-and-modes methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Tokens MUST be organized in exactly three layers: reference (raw palette/scale), system (semantic, mode-aware), component (component-scoped). Component-layer tokens MUST resolve via the system layer; direct component → reference references are forbidden.

rationale.source: Nathan Curtis — Naming Tokens in Design Systems

rule.rationale: The system layer is the isolation boundary that makes theme swaps cheap. Jumping component → reference defeats the abstraction; rebrands then require N component edits.

rule.statement: Each semantic token MUST encode exactly one intent. Splitting brand and interactive roles is mandatory (e.g., `brand.primary` vs `action.primary`).
rule.rationale: Dual-purpose tokens couple unrelated concerns; a contrast fix for the action color must not change the brand color.

rule.statement: Every semantic token MUST have a value in every required mode of its collection (e.g., light, dark, high-contrast, brand). Missing mode values MUST fail the CI build.
rule.rationale: Missing mode values fall back to default at runtime — invisible until a user switches theme. Build-time enforcement catches the gap at authoring.

rule.statement: Token source files MUST use the W3C Design Tokens Community Group format (`$value`, `$type`). Proprietary token formats are forbidden in the source-of-truth files.
rule.rationale: DTCG is the emerging standard; adopting it now keeps Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, and Specify interoperable and avoids migration cost when tools converge.

rule.statement: Component code MUST NOT contain raw hex values or direct references to reference tokens. Lint MUST reject `#RRGGBB` and `var(--color-blue-500)`-style direct reference-token usage in component files.
rule.rationale: Raw values and direct reference-token usage bypass the semantic layer and break on theme switch. Automated lint is the only reliable enforcement.

rule.statement: The token pipeline CI run MUST snapshot every mode in visual regression (Chromatic / Percy / Loki). A diff in any mode without an explicit "expected" annotation blocks merge.
rule.rationale: Mode-swap regressions are invisible in code diff; only visual comparison catches an accidental color swap.
